questions.text: When did the Mughal emperor appoint the East India Company as the Diwan of Bengal?
questions.explanation: The Diwani was granted on 12 August 1765, making the Company the chief financial administrator of the territory.

questions.text: As Diwan, what was the primary concern of the Company?
options: Organizing revenue resources to yield enough to meet expenses
questions.explanation: The Company wanted a large revenue income but was unwilling to set up a regular system of assessment and collection.

questions.text: Why did the economy of Bengal face a deep crisis after the Company became Diwan?
options: Low production and high revenue demands
Peasants left to work in factories
questions.explanation: Artisans were deserting villages because they were being forced to sell their goods at low prices.

questions.text: Who introduced the 'Permanent Settlement' in 1793?
questions.explanation: Lord Cornwallis was the Governor-General of India at the time.

questions.text: Under the Permanent Settlement, who were recognized as Zamindars?
questions.explanation: They were asked to collect rent from the peasants and pay revenue to the Company.

questions.text: What was the main feature of the Permanent Settlement?
options: The amount to be paid was fixed permanently
Revenue was based on soil quality
questions.explanation: The fixed revenue was intended to encourage Zamindars to invest in improving the land.

questions.text: What happened if a Zamindar failed to pay the fixed revenue?
questions.explanation: Numerous zamindaris were sold off at auctions organized by the Company.

questions.text: In the early 19th century, Holt Mackenzie devised a new system called:
questions.explanation: This system was introduced in the North Western Provinces of the Bengal Presidency.

questions.text: In the Mahalwari system, what does 'Mahal' refer to?
options: A village or a group of villages
questions.explanation: The revenue was to be collected from the village collectively.

questions.text: Who was responsible for paying the revenue in the Mahalwari system?
questions.explanation: The charge of collecting revenue and paying it to the Company was given to the village headman.

questions.text: The 'Munro System' was another name for:
questions.explanation: It was developed by Thomas Munro and gradually extended all over South India.

questions.text: In the Ryotwari system, the revenue was settled directly with the:
questions.explanation: The British argued that in the south there were no traditional zamindars.

questions.text: Why were the new revenue systems problematic for the peasants?
options: The British forced them to move to cities
questions.explanation: The high revenue driven by a desire to increase income led to peasants fleeing the countryside.

questions.text: Why was there a high demand for Indian Indigo in Europe during the 18th century?
options: It produced a rich blue color preferred by cloth manufacturers
questions.explanation: Woad, the European alternative, produced a pale and dull blue.

questions.text: Indigo can be grown only in ________ regions.
questions.explanation: India's climate was perfect for large-scale indigo cultivation.

questions.text: The two main systems of indigo cultivation in India were:
questions.explanation: In Nij, the planter produced indigo on land he directly controlled.

questions.text: In the 'Ryoti' system of indigo cultivation, planters forced the ryots to:
options: Sign a contract (satta) to grow indigo on at least 25% of their land
questions.explanation: The system trapped peasants in a cycle of debt.

questions.text: What was the main environmental problem with indigo cultivation?
options: It had deep roots and exhausted the soil fertility rapidly
questions.explanation: After an indigo harvest, the land could not be used for sowing rice.

questions.text: When did the 'Blue Rebellion' break out in Bengal?
questions.explanation: Thousands of ryots in Bengal refused to grow indigo and attacked factories.

questions.text: Who were the 'Gomasthas'?
options: Agents of indigo planters who collected rent
questions.explanation: They were often beaten by villagers during the rebellion.

questions.text: The Indigo Commission (1860) held the ________ guilty for the plight of ryots.
questions.explanation: It declared that indigo cultivation was not profitable for ryots.

questions.text: After the indigo production collapsed in Bengal, the planters shifted their operation to:
questions.explanation: The discovery of synthetic dyes later affected this new production area too.

questions.text: Mahatma Gandhi’s visit to ________ in 1917 marked the beginning of the movement against indigo planters.
questions.explanation: He was persuaded by a peasant from Bihar to see the plight of the indigo cultivators there.

questions.text: A 'Bigha' is a unit of:
questions.explanation: The size of this unit varied in different parts of India.

questions.text: Who was the Indigo planter who helped the British during the 1857 revolt?
options: The British worried that the indigo rebellion would merge with the 1857 revolt
questions.explanation: The memory of 1857 made the British government very nervous about any rural uprising.

questions.text: Which color is produced from the woad plant?
questions.explanation: Woad was the plant used in Europe before the widespread use of Indian Indigo.

questions.text: Colonialism forced Indian farmers to grow 'crops for Europe' like jute, tea, and:
questions.explanation: The British used various methods to expand the cultivation of crops they needed.

questions.text: The Permanent Settlement created a class of ________ loyal to the British.
questions.explanation: The British hoped that fixing revenue would create stable allies.

questions.text: Who were 'Lathiyals'?
options: Strongmen with clubs maintained by planters
questions.explanation: They were used by planters to intimidate ryots who refused to grow indigo.

questions.text: The British system in South India (Ryotwari) was different because:
questions.explanation: Read and Munro felt they had to deal directly with the cultivators.

questions.text: The word 'Vat' in the context of Indigo refers to:
questions.explanation: In indigo production, the plant went through different vats for soaking and beating.

questions.text: Slave Revolt in St Domingue (Caribbean) in 1791 affected the supply of:
questions.explanation: The collapse of production in French colonies led to the search for other sources like India.

questions.text: What was the 'Satta'?
questions.explanation: Planters pressured village headmen to sign 'Sattas' on behalf of ryots.

questions.text: Why did the 'Blue Rebellion' receive support from some local Zamindars?
options: They were unhappy with the increasing power of planters
questions.explanation: Many zamindars resented being forced by planters to give them land on long leases.

questions.text: The Lieutenant Governor toured the indigo districts in the winter of 1859, which was seen by ryots as:
options: A sign of sympathy for their plight
questions.explanation: Ryots interpreted the tour as a sign that the government supported them against the planters.

questions.text: Ashley Eden was:
options: A magistrate who issued a notice in favor of ryots
questions.explanation: He issued a notice saying that ryots would not be compelled to accept indigo contracts.

questions.text: What was the result of the high revenue demand in the Munro system?
options: Peasants fled the countryside and villages became deserted
questions.explanation: The 'optimistic' British officials' hope that peasants would become 'rich enterprising farmers' failed.

questions.text: Under the Permanent Settlement, the revenue was not increased even if:
questions.explanation: This meant the state did not benefit from the increased production in later years.

questions.text: Which system required the periodic revision of revenue?
questions.explanation: Unlike the Permanent Settlement, Mahalwari revenue was not fixed forever.

questions.text: The main problem for the British in the Nij system was:
options: Difficulty in expanding the area under cultivation
questions.explanation: Planters found it hard to get enough land and labor for large plantations.
